terrainNotes.en: Long first day. Leaves Porto along the Douro riverfront to Foz, then tracks the Atlantic coast north through Matosinhos, Leça da Palmeira, and a long succession of wooden boardwalks (passadiços) past Perafita, Labruge, and Vila Chã to Vila do Conde. Mostly flat with constant ocean views, but exposed to Atlantic wind and sun. Many pilgrims split this stage at Matosinhos (~10 km) or Labruge (~20 km).

narrative.en: You leave the Sé do Porto westward, descending through the old city toward the Douro and Foz, where the river meets the Atlantic. At Foz you make a choice the Central pilgrims do not: you turn right along the ocean. The boardwalks begin almost immediately and run for hours. Waves on your left, dunes and pine on your right, and the rhythm of your feet on timber. By late afternoon you cross the Ave river into Vila do Conde, a fishing and shipbuilding town whose pilgrim tradition is as old as the Central route. The day is long but the ocean never leaves you.

commonExperiences.en: The first sight of the Atlantic at Foz — the moment the Camino turns maritime
Hours of boardwalk walking, the sound of the surf constant in the background
Sea spray, salt on the lips, wind that pushes from the north
Blisters earlier than expected — the boardwalks are flat but the day is long
The convent and aqueduct of Santa Clara rising above Vila do Conde

reflection.en: You chose the sea over the inland way. What were you hoping the water would give you that the hills would not?

terrainNotes.en: Almost entirely flat. More boardwalks past Mindelo, Vila Chã de Areia, and Apúlia, interrupted by short stretches of actual beach walking. Cross the Cávado river by pedestrian bridge into Esposende. The Senda Litoral (strict beachfront variant) runs parallel for most of this stage. Limited shade — start early in summer.

narrative.en: The second day is where the Coastal Camino teaches you what it actually is. Yesterday was a turn; today is a commitment. You walk past fishermen mending nets on the beach at Mindelo, past the windmills of Apúlia, across the long wooden walkways where the Atlantic stretches to the horizon on one side and small dune-garden plots hide on the other. The Portuguese call this stretch the Costa Verde — green coast — and in April or September the Atlantic haar rolls in for hours and turns everything grey and hushed. By afternoon you cross the Cávado into Esposende, a quiet town where most pilgrims are outnumbered by weekend beachgoers. The first soreness shows up in the hips and shoulders, not the feet. Your body is learning.

commonExperiences.en: Windmills at Apúlia — the only ones on any Camino
Walking on actual beach sand for the first time
Atlantic fog that muffles sound and hides the horizon
Recognizing the same faces from yesterday's boardwalks
The first evening in a small Portuguese town where pilgrims are a minority

reflection.en: Your body has started adapting without asking permission. What is it telling you that your head has not admitted yet?

terrainNotes.en: The landscape begins to shift. Past Marinhas and Castelo do Neiva the path climbs a low coastal ridge through pine forest — the first real climb on the route — before dropping back to the Atlantic at Chafé. The approach to Viana do Castelo crosses the long Eiffel Bridge (Ponte Eiffel, 1878) over the Lima river — built by Eiffel & Cie, the firm Gustave Eiffel co-founded with his partner Théophile Seyrig in 1868. Viana is the largest town since Porto and the cultural heart of the Minho region.

narrative.en: Today the Atlantic shows you that it has moods. The first half of the stage is still the boardwalk you know — surf on the left, pines on the right — but after Castelo do Neiva the path turns inland and climbs into a coastal forest. For the first time since Porto you lose sight of the sea. The pine resin smells clean and sharp and the trail is soft underfoot. When you drop back down to the coast at Chafé the Atlantic seems to greet you with a colder wind. You walk into Viana do Castelo across the Eiffel Bridge (Ponte Eiffel) — completed in 1878 by Gustave Eiffel's firm Eiffel & Cie — and the city opens up around you. The Basilica of Santa Luzia watches from the hill above, visible from most of the old town. Viana is where most pilgrims take a rest day.

commonExperiences.en: The first climb that makes the legs notice the pack weight
Pine forest after days of open coast — the shift in smell is dramatic
Crossing the Eiffel bridge with the river Lima beneath and the Monte de Santa Luzia above
Arriving in a city and feeling both relief and reluctance
Climbing (or taking the funicular) to the Basilica for the view

reflection.en: The sea finally let you go inland for a few hours. Did you miss it, or was the silence a relief?

terrainNotes.en: The longest stage of the Portuguese section and arguably the most beautiful. Leaves Viana along the coast, passes Carreço and Afife with their long Atlantic beaches, crosses a series of small coastal headlands, then reaches Âncora and finally Caminha at the mouth of the Miño river. The views across the Miño to Spain become visible near the end. Caminha is the last Portuguese town on the route.

narrative.en: Today the Camino gives you its most generous day. You walk north from Viana with the sea close on your left for hours. At Afife the beach is so long you lose track of distance. Near Vila Praia de Âncora, the Dólmen da Barrosa sits hidden in the pine forest — a Neolithic megalithic monument older than any cathedral, declared a Portuguese National Monument in 1910. By afternoon the coast begins to bend westward and the first glimpse of Spain appears across the water — the cone of Monte de Santa Trega rising out of the sea. Beyond it inland is the Serra da Groba, and beneath the mountain is A Guarda, tomorrow's destination. You arrive in Caminha in the late afternoon. The town is small, walled, and utterly quiet in the evening. The river crossing is tomorrow morning. You stand on the riverbank and look across the Minho and for the first time the journey feels real, because the border is visible.

commonExperiences.en: The beach at Afife — flat, endless, hypnotic
The Dólmen da Barrosa near Vila Praia de Âncora — Neolithic, older than the pilgrimage itself
First sight of Spain across the Miño, Monte de Santa Trega rising from A Guarda
The quiet of Caminha in the evening — a border town that empties after dark
Checking the ferry schedule three times before going to bed

reflection.en: The other country is close enough to see. What did you think the border would feel like, and what does it actually feel like now that it is in front of you?

name.pt: Caminha a A Guarda (travessia do ferry)

terrainNotes.en: Short transitional stage centred on the river crossing. Walk from the Caminha waterfront to the boat dock (~500 m), cross the Minho/Miño by small boat (~10 minutes, ~€6), then walk from the A Guarda dock into the town centre (~1.5 km). The original municipal ferry (Santa Rita de Cássia) has been suspended since 2020; two private operators — Xacobeo Transfer (~07:30-15:30) and Taxi Boat Peregrinos (~07:00-17:00 every 30 minutes) — now run the crossing in peak April-October season, with much reduced winter schedules (1-2 morning crossings). Pilgrims who miss the last boat overnight in Caminha or detour ~30 km inland to cross by the Tui international bridge. This is the end of the Portuguese section in this dataset release.

narrative.en: The boat is small — room for maybe ten pilgrims and a bicycle or two. The crossing takes about ten minutes. In the middle of the river you are neither in Portugal nor in Spain and the current pulls slightly east toward the open Atlantic. Monte de Santa Trega rises from the Spanish bank, crowned with a Celtic castro whose circular stone foundations are about two thousand years old. When you step off the boat at A Guarda you step onto Galician soil for the first time — a land that speaks its own language, that feels closer to Santiago than anywhere you have been so far. For most pilgrims this is the shortest walking day of the whole Camino, and the most symbolically weighted. This dataset release ends here. The walk onward through Oia, Baiona, Vigo, and Redondela to merge with the Central route is planned for a future release; for now, A Guarda is the border post and the pause.

commonExperiences.en: Waiting at the Caminha ferry dock with other pilgrims, checking watches
The moment the Portuguese coast slides behind you and the Spanish coast slides forward
Stepping off the boat in A Guarda and hearing Galician for the first time
Seeing Monte de Santa Trega up close — the Celtic castro unmistakable
Eating fresh lobster or percebes in A Guarda — the town's signature

reflection.en: You crossed a border by boat instead of by bridge. What does the water have to say about borders that the land does not?
